preFix.title: Wet-felted phone case
preFix.subtitle: A slim merino sleeve for a standard smartphone, approximately 13 by 7 cm finished, felted without a separate resist
preFix.excerpt: Wet-felt a snug phone sleeve from merino roving using a folded-wool construction that avoids the need for a separate resist. The fold line creates the pocket opening. Starting dimensions of 18 by 10 cm shrink to approximately 13 by 7 cm after fulling, giving a close fit for a standard smartphone. The single-piece method keeps the construction simple and the seams minimal.
preFix.sourceNotes: Synthesised from the standard wet-felting flat-panel tradition. Fold-line phone sleeve construction is common introductory guild practice; the method is observation-led.

content.text: A wet-felted phone sleeve is made from a single flat panel of merino

that is folded in half before wetting, so the fold line becomes the closed base of the pocket. No separate resist is needed. Starting at 18 by 10 cm, the panel shrinks to approximately 13 by 7 cm during

, giving a close fit for a standard smartphone. Merino shrinks roughly 25 to 30 per cent in each direction; if your phone is larger, scale the starting dimensions up proportionally. Two or three cross-hatched layers of thin roving on each face gives a panel dense enough to protect the screen without being too thick to slide in and out of a pocket.

items.name: Olive-oil soap bar or mild wool wash
Hot water (as hot as your hands can bear)

content.text: Step 1: Lay out the roving. Place the felting mat on the worktop and lay the cotton tea towel on top. Pull thin wisps of merino roving and lay them in a rectangle 18 by 10 cm on the towel. The first layer runs horizontally across the width; the second layer runs vertically, crossing the first at 90 degrees. Add a third layer horizontally if you want a denser panel. Each layer should be thin enough to be translucent when held to a light.

Step 2: Fold and wet down. Fold the roving rectangle in half along the short axis to give a folded panel of 9 by 10 cm. Dissolve a small amount of olive-oil soap in hot water to make a light suds. Pour the soapy water slowly over the folded layout from the centre outward to saturate both layers without disturbing the arrangement. Press down gently through the tea towel to help the water penetrate.

Step 3: Begin rubbing. Rub the surface with flat hands in circular motions for 5 minutes, keeping the surface hot and adding more soapy water as needed. Work both faces equally, flipping carefully every few minutes. The fold line at the base should stay closed; press it gently to encourage the fibres there to bond. Do not rub the top open edge too aggressively at this stage, or the two faces will seal together above the intended opening.

Step 4: Full to size. Once the surface holds together without the layers peeling apart, begin fulling by rolling the panel inside the tea towel and rolling it back and forth on the felting mat with firm pressure for 3 to 4 minutes. Unroll, rotate the panel 90 degrees, re-roll, and continue. Check the dimensions every few minutes. Stop rolling when the panel measures approximately 13 by 7 cm. The two open side edges will have sealed slightly during rubbing; check that the top opening remains open and the internal pocket is accessible.

Step 5: Open the top edge. Once at the intended size, use sharp scissors to trim the top opening to a clean straight edge if the two faces have bonded there. Trim the side edges if needed to square up the opening. Insert your phone to check fit while the felt is still wet and slightly pliable; the felt will dry to whatever shape it is left in.

Step 6: Rinse and dry. Rinse the case in hot water to remove all soap, then plunge into cold water for 30 seconds. Press flat between both palms to extract water. Insert a folded piece of cardboard cut to the phone dimensions and leave the case to dry around it so the opening stays square. Remove the cardboard once fully dry.

items.fix: Trim across the sealed edge with sharp scissors after fulling to reopen the pocket. To prevent this on future attempts, keep rubbing pressure light at the top edge in the first 10 minutes and work the body of the panel more.
items.cause: The two faces were rubbed together at the open edge before the fibres had oriented parallel to the surface
items.problem: The top opening has sealed shut

items.fix: Re-wet in hot soapy water and continue rolling and rubbing until the case shrinks further. Check the size every 3 minutes. Merino can continue to shrink well past the initial finished estimate with continued agitation.
items.cause: Insufficient fulling or the starting dimensions were too large
items.problem: The case is too loose for the phone

items.fix: When laying out the roving, keep the layers thinner in the central 1 to 2 cm that will become the fold line. After wetting, press the fold flat and rub it directly with hot soapy hands before folding the panel.
items.cause: The roving at the fold was thicker than the rest of the panel
items.problem: The fold-line base has a visible ridge or lump

items.fix: Stop fulling as soon as the planned dimensions are reached. The case should feel firm but still have some give. If already over-fulled, the stiffness will ease slightly after a few uses.
items.cause: Over-fulling has compacted the felt to an unusually dense fabric
items.problem: The case is too stiff to slide a phone in easily

content.text: Hand-wash in cool water with a small drop of wool wash if the case becomes marked. Press flat between dry towels and dry with the phone (or a same-size card template) inside to keep the shape. Do not tumble-dry or machine-wash; the case will shrink further. Felt is naturally water-resistant to light splashes but is not waterproof; remove the phone before washing the case. If the opening stretches with use, re-wet the case and allow it to dry without anything inside so the felt contracts back.

initialFindings.message: Tutorial PUBLISHED with heroMediaId IS NULL — hero-fill must be run
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
